title: Today's Tech Brief: Deus ex machina: Half of US Christians trust AI's spiritual advice
summary: A look at the intersection of AI and spirituality, with a focus on US Christians trusting AI's spiritual advice.
imageAlt: Image from source article for Deus ex machina: Half of US Christians trust AI's spiritual advice
imageCaption: Deus ex machina: Half of US Christians trust AI's spiritual advice
imageSourceTitle: Deus ex machina: Half of US Christians trust AI's spiritual advice

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to Today's Tech Brief. I've been digging into some fascinating developments at the intersection of technology and spirituality. It seems that AI is making inroads in unexpected places – like the pews.

According to a recent report, half of US Christians are open to receiving spiritual advice from AI. This raises interesting questions about the evolving role of technology in our personal lives. I am looking at the potential implications of AI sycophancy and spirituality – a combination that's got me thinking about the phrase 'deus ex machina'. The idea that AI could provide guidance and support is certainly intriguing, and it's not hard to see why some people might be drawn to it.

Another related story that's been on my mind is the growing trend of virtual church services. With more and more people turning to online platforms for community and connection, it's no surprise that spiritual leaders are exploring new ways to reach their congregations. And then there's the news about AI-powered meditation apps, which promise to help users find inner peace and calm. It's clear that technology is changing the way we approach spirituality, and I'm curious to see where this trend will lead.

Deus ex machina: Half of US Christians trust AI's spiritual advice is also worth a quick mention. America’s Christian population appears to have found God in precisely the place you’d expect a manifestation of the divine to b... The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.

My quick read is that today's set of stories is useful because it gives us texture, not a sermon. Each item has its own little job: one tells us what changed, another tells us what people are worried about, and another gives the day its mood.
So that is the brief version: a few headlines, a little context, and just enough connective tissue to make the day easier to follow without turning the whole thing into homework.
